topic.what: Overview of ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) as an adaptogen with documented gastroprotective effects in animal models, mediated by withanolides with anti-stress and anti-inflammatory properties.
topic.how: Narrative review synthesizing in-vivo animal model studies and phytochemical analyses of ashwagandha's active constituents (withanolides, alkaloids, sitoindosides), mapping their biological activities to adaptogenic and GI protective outcomes.
topic.why_matters: Chronic psychological stress is a recognized trigger and exacerbator of gastritis; ashwagandha's dual action as an adaptogen and gastroprotective agent offers a mechanistic bridge connecting the stress-gastritis axis to herbal intervention, relevant for holistic gastritis management.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Reduction of stress biomarkers (cortisol, corticosterone) and normalization of stress-induced physiological dysregulation
characteristics.value: Well-documented adaptogenic effects in animal stress models
characteristics.evidence: Multiple in-vivo studies using forced swim, immobilization, and cold stress models reviewed
characteristics.context: Animal model data; human clinical evidence for HPA axis modulation exists but is less extensive

characteristics.what_it_measures: Reduction in stress-induced gastric ulceration scores and mucosal damage indices
characteristics.value: Significant gastroprotection in stress-ulcer animal models
characteristics.evidence: In-vivo animal studies showing reduced ulcer index with ashwagandha extract administration
characteristics.context: In-vivo animal data only; clinical gastritis trials with ashwagandha are absent from this review

characteristics.what_it_measures: Inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokines and mediators contributing to gastric mucosal inflammation
characteristics.value: Withanolides demonstrate NF-κB and COX pathway inhibition
characteristics.evidence: Phytochemical activity data for key withanolides reviewed
characteristics.context: Primarily in-vitro mechanistic data; in-vivo inflammatory gastritis models less characterized

characteristics.what_it_measures: Mechanistic linkage between ashwagandha's anti-stress effects and gastric mucosal protection
characteristics.value: Conceptually well-supported; stress-HPA-gastric acid axis is a recognized mechanism
characteristics.evidence: Pathophysiological reasoning connecting adaptogen activity to GI protection; supported by stress-ulcer model results
characteristics.context: Causal chain plausible but not directly demonstrated in clinical gastritis populations

good_for: Stress-related or psychosomatic gastritis where HPA axis dysregulation is implicated
Integrative gastritis management combining mucosal protection with stress reduction
Investigating withanolides as leads for novel gastroprotective agents
Ayurvedic/traditional medicine research and ethnopharmacological documentation
not_for: H. pylori-positive gastritis as primary or sole treatment
Replacement of established acid suppression therapy without clinical evidence
Patients with thyroid disorders or on immunosuppressants without medical supervision
requires: Standardized withanolide content in extract preparations (typically ≥5% withanolides)
Clinical trials in gastritis patients to establish human efficacy
Screening for contraindications: pregnancy, thyroid disease, autoimmune conditions

novelty: Articulated the mechanistic bridge between ashwagandha's adaptogenic and gastroprotective properties, linking the stress-gastritis axis to herbal pharmacology
Consolidated in-vivo gastroprotective evidence for ashwagandha in a single review
Framed ashwagandha as a multi-target agent addressing both the psychological trigger (stress) and the GI outcome (mucosal damage) of stress-induced gastritis
open_problems: Clinical RCTs in human gastritis populations are absent; translation from animal models unvalidated
Optimal dose, formulation, and duration for gastroprotective effects in humans undefined
Interaction between ashwagandha and H. pylori eradication regimens not studied
Relative contribution of adaptogenic vs direct gastroprotective mechanisms to observed protection unresolved
